coredata.dc:title: Development of sustainable and active food packaging films based on alginate enriched with plant polyphenol carbon dots and layered clay
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Mao, Long
Dong, Zhiye
Dong, Fang
Dai, Xianglong
Cao, Zhiming
coredata.dc:description: Natural polymer based food packaging has attracted more and more attention, but the lack of active functions of natural polymer hinders its application in the field of active packaging. In this study, chlorogenic acid carbon dots (CGA-CDs) was synthesized mildly using natural plant polyphenol CGA as carbon source, and CGA functionalized layered clays (LDHs@CGA) was introduced as reinforcing agent. Alg active films were fabricated by solution casting method using natural polysaccharide-alginate (Alg), CGA-CDs and LDHs@CGA. The results showed that the average particle size of the synthesized CGA-CDs was ∼2.0 nm, with obvious graphitized structure inside and active groups (-OH and -NH2, etc.) outside, showing obvious UV absorption and antibacterial activity. With the addition of CGA-CDs, UV barrier, antibacterial, thermal stability, hydrophobicity and flexibility of Alg active films were significantly improved. When the amount of CGA-CDs reached 2 %, UV transmission (280 nm) and of Alg active films was only 2.66 %, which was reduced by 88.1 % compared with pure Alg film. The highest antibacterial rate against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus reached 86.0 % and 91.6 %, respectively. Freshness preservation experiment of fresh-cut apples indicated that Alg active coating incorporating CGA-CDs maintained the lowest browning index and mildew. These results indicate that the excellent active functions of CGA-CDs can promote the application of Alg matrix in the field of natural polymer active packaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chlorogenic acid
Carbon dots
Layered clay
Alginate
Active packaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical property and cytotoxicity of CGA-CDs
Chemical structure of Alg active films
Optical property of Alg active films
Thermal stability of Alg active films
Surface morphology and properties of Alg active films
Mechanical properties of Alg active films
Antibacterial activities of Alg active films
Freshness preservation of Alg active films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) TEM image (inset: HRTEM image) and (c) the corresponding particle size distribution histogram of CGA-CDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra, (b) UV–Vis spectra, (c) XPS survey spectrum, (d) C 1S spectra, (e) O 1S spectra and (f) N 1S spectra of CGA-CDs.

(a) PL emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths, (b) UV–Vis absorption, maximum PL excitation and emission spectra of CGA-CDs, digital photos of CGA-CDs under sunlight and UV lamp (365 nm) irradiation. (c) Cell viability of CGA-CDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra, (b) UV–Vis light transmittance curves and (c) digital photos of Alg active films.
(a) TG and (b) DTG curves of Alg active films.
SEM images of surface for (a) Alg, (b) LA, (c) CLA-1 and (d) CLA-2.
(a) Water contact angle, (b) elongation at break and (c) tensile strength of Alg active films (Different superscript letters indicate significant differences among films (P < 0.05)).
(a) Antibacterial activity against E. coli and (b) digital photos of antibacterial test for LDHs@CGA, CGA-CDs and Alg active films.
Digital photos of fresh-cut apples at different storage times.
Surface color index of fresh-cut apples treated with CLA coatings at different storage time.
Optical parameters of Alg active films (Different superscript letters indicate significant differences among films (P < 0.05)).
Color parameters of Alg active films (Different superscript letters indicate significant differences among films (P < 0.05)).
The thermal analysis results of Alg active films.

head.ce:title: Development of sustainable and active food packaging films based on alginate enriched with plant polyphenol carbon dots and layered clay

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications, Xiamen University of Technology, Xiamen 361024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications, Xiamen University of Technology, Xiamen 361024, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xiamen Meijiamei New Material Technology Co., Ltd., Xiamen 361110, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Xiamen Meijiamei New Material Technology Co., Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xiamen Meijiamei New Material Technology Co., Ltd., Xiamen 361110, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Natural polymer based food packaging has attracted more and more attention, but the lack of active functions of natural polymer hinders its application in the field of active packaging. In this study, chlorogenic acid carbon dots (CGA-CDs) was synthesized mildly using natural plant polyphenol CGA as carbon source, and CGA functionalized layered clays (LDHs@CGA) was introduced as reinforcing agent. Alg active films were fabricated by solution casting method using natural polysaccharide-alginate (Alg), CGA-CDs and LDHs@CGA. The results showed that the average particle size of the synthesized CGA-CDs was ∼2.0 nm, with obvious graphitized structure inside and active groups (-OH and -NH2, etc.) outside, showing obvious UV absorption and antibacterial activity. With the addition of CGA-CDs, UV barrier, antibacterial, thermal stability, hydrophobicity and flexibility of Alg active films were significantly improved. When the amount of CGA-CDs reached 2 %, UV transmission (280 nm) and of Alg active films was only 2.66 %, which was reduced by 88.1 % compared with pure Alg film. The highest antibacterial rate against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus reached 86.0 % and 91.6 %, respectively. Freshness preservation experiment of fresh-cut apples indicated that Alg active coating incorporating CGA-CDs maintained the lowest browning index and mildew. These results indicate that the excellent active functions of CGA-CDs can promote the application of Alg matrix in the field of natural polymer active packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety issues are increasingly receiving widespread attention, and the food packaging industry, as an important link in packaging safety, is receiving widespread attention [1]. However, most of the current food packaging materials are mainly non degradable plastics (such as polyethylene, polypropylene, nylon, etc.), and the use and disposal of these non-degradable packaging materials will deepen the impact of plastic pollution on the ecological environment [2,3]. Searching for biodegradable food packaging substrates that can solve the environmental pollution caused by packaging materials from the source. Biomass resources have a wide range of sources and are cheap, providing more selectivity for the substitution of biodegradable packaging materials [2]. Biobased polymer materials can be made entirely from biological resources such as natural polymers extracted from plants and marine organisms, and produced by microorganisms through fermentation processes [4,5]. These include polysaccharides, proteins, lipids, polylactic acid, etc. Seaweeds can be utilized as food contact materials as a potentially active agent, polysaccharide rich raw material, or extracts [6]. Alginate (Alg) is an anionic polysaccharide extracted from brown seaweed, characterized by biodegradability, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, renewability, abundance, and good film-forming ability [7]. However, in the field of active food packaging materials, it is still necessary to add functional substances and enhancers to Alg matrix to achieve better active functions (antibacterial activity, UV barrier, etc.) and usability (mechanical properties, stability, etc.).

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of carbon based material nanomaterial, have attracted widespread research attention in recent years [8]. Based on their unique optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, low cost, and easy functionalization, they have been widely used in fields such as optoelectronic materials and biomedical engineering [9–11]. Different synthesis processes and precursors lead to different structures and properties, but they can all be regarded as core-shell structures [12]. The shell layer can be easily passivated or functionalized by organic groups, which is particularly important for the unique properties of CDs. The surface functional groups also have notable antibacterial properties, making CDs targeted and selective [9]. CDs, as a type of active nanoparticles with a special structure, have been studied in the reinforcement and modification of polymer matrix [9]. Riahi et al. [13] studied the effect of SQDs (sulfur quantum dots) on the Alg based film. The results indicated that UV barrier property and tensile strength of composite film were increased by 82 % and 18 % compared with Alg based film. Also, the addition of SQDs to Alg based film did not affect the stiffness and water vapor barrier permeability. This reveals that CDs have great research potential in the field of active packaging. The diversity of carbon source provides more possibilities for improving the performance of CDs. Plant polyphenols, as a natural active substance, have received widespread attention in the field of active packaging [12,14]. The synthesis of CDs using plant polyphenols can leverage the characteristics of both CDs and plant polyphenols, resulting in the preparation of CDs with superior activity and functionality [12]. The high reactivity of natural polyphenols, one of the typical widespread biomass materials in nature, might be able to ensure the facile and modular fabrication of numerous functional materials without using complex organic synthetic procedures, chemical solvents, and external energy consumption, which are necessary in the established methods [9,12]. Among these natural polyphenols, chlorogenic acid (CGA) is one of the main antibacterial components of honeysuckle, and it can also be isolated from many vegetables and fruits, such as green coffee beans, potatoes, apples, blueberries and pears [15,16]. It can be seen from the current literature reports on the modification of Alg with CDs, there are few reports on the use of plant polyphenols as carbon sources to prepare CDs for enhancing of Alg matrix [13,17,18]. Therefore, using CGA as carbon sources to synthesize CDs can combine the high biological activity of CGA with the characteristics of CDs. The introduction of highly active biomass-derived CDs is one of the important focuses of our research.

Meanwhile, layered clay (montmorillonite, bentonite, Layered double hydroxides (LDHs), halloysite, etc.) has been widely studied in the field of modified polymer matrix due to its layered structure, high rigidity and stability [19,20]. Naidu et al. studied the effects of layered clay (1–5 wt%) on the mechanical, optical, thermal, solubility, water absorption, and water vapor permeability (WVP) of xylan/Alg composite films, and applied them to food packaging materials [21]. Due to the impermeability of layered clay (bentonite and halloysite), WVP of composite films decreases by 49 % when the load of either bentonite or halloysite was 5 wt%. The presence of layered clay had no significant effect on the thermal stability and solubility of composite films, while compared with pure xylan/Alg film, the optical properties of composite films have been significantly improved. In our previous work, a simple and green method has been reported to prepare natural polyphenols containing active catechol functionalized layered clay nanosheets (LDHs), such as LDHs@tannin-Fe3+ and LDHs@anthocyanin-Cu2+ [22,23]. This modification method can not only increase the interfacial compatibility between LDHs and polymer matrix, thereby better utilizing the natural barrier properties and enhancing effects of LDHs nanosheets, but also achieve effective loading of natural plant polyphenols, endowing LDHs with antibacterial and antioxidant activities. Therefore, the introduction of CGA functionalized LDHs (LDHs@CGA) is also one of the important focuses of our research.

Therefore, based on the above analysis, this study introduced CGA as a carbon source to synthesize CGA-CDs, and CGA was also used to functionalize LDHs to prepare CGA functionalized LDHs (LDHs@CGA). For the first time, CGA-CDs and LDHs@CGA were applied in the modification of Alg matrix. Chemical structure, optical, surface, thermal stability, mechanical, and antibacterial properties of Alg active films with different amounts of CGA-CDs were investigated. Furthermore, the preservation experiment of the corresponding Alg active coatings on fresh-cut apples was further explored for applications in the field of active packaging.

Chlorogenic acid (CGA, purity = 95 %) and glycerin (purity ≥99.7 %) were provided by Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Sodium alginate (Alg, light yellow powder, purity≥90 %, M/G = 1:2), Poly(ethylenimine) (PEI, Mw = 1800, purity = 99 %) and CuCl2·2H2O (purity = 99.99 %) were purchased from Makclin (Shanghai, China). Tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane hydrochloride (Tris-HCl) buffer solution (10 mM, pH 8.5) was purchased from Phygene Biotechnology (China). Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) was synthesized by hydrothermal method using magnesium salt, aluminum salt, and urea as raw materials [24]. Average size and thickness of LDHs nanosheets were ∼ 1500 nm and ∼ 40 nm, respectively [22]. In our recently published research [25], LDHs@CGA was successfully synthesized by the deposition and complexation of CGA and Cu2+ on the surface of LDHs. Apples were bought from local fresh supermarket (Xiamen Sungiven Holding Foods Co., Ltd., China; grown in the New Zealand). All purchased reagents were for direct use.

Generally, 200 mg of CGA and 200 mg of PEI were successively added to 50 mL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH 8.5). Subsequently, the above mixture was stirred at room temperature for 12 h. The reaction solution was filtered through a hydrophilic filter (pore size = 0.22 μm, poly(ethersulfone), Tianjin Jintang) and dialyzed for 48 h with a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off 3500 Da, Beijing solabo (China)). The synthesis and purification of CGA-CDs need to be protected from light. Finally, the purified dark brown CGA-CDs were freeze-dried and stored away from light for further use. The characterization methods and synthesis schematic illustration (Fig. S1) of CGA-CDs were described in the Supplementary Information.

Firstly, a certain amount of CGA-CDs (0, 0.0053 g (0.5 %), 0.0105 g (1 %), and 0.021 g (2 %), based on the mass of Alg) and 0.0105 g of LDHs@CGA (1 %, based on the mass of Alg) were uniformly dispersed in 50 mL of deionized water and ultrasounded for about 10 min until LDHs@CGA and CGA-CDs were evenly dispersed. Then, 1.05 g of Alg powder and 0.315 g of glycerin (30 %, based on the mass of Alg) was were successively added to the above dispersions. The above dispersion was heated to 90 °C and stirred for 1 h to obtain a uniform and transparent film-forming solution. Then, the film-forming solution was sonicated for 5 min to remove bubbles, and poured into a horizontally placed poly(tetrafluoroethylene) mold (24 cm × 30 cm), and dried in a drying oven at 40 °C for 24 h to obtain Alg active films. According to the addition of CGA in the Alg active films (0, 0.5 %, 1 % and 2 %, based on the mass of Alg), the final Alg active films were marked as Alg, LA (Alg + 1 % LDHs@CGA), CLA-0.5 (Alg + 1 % LDHs@CGA + 0.5 % CGA-CDs), CLA-1 (Alg + 1 % LDHs@CGA + 1 % CGA-CDs) and CLA-2 (Alg + 1 % LDHs@CGA + 2 % CGA-CDs), respectively. The composition and naming of Alg active films were presented in Table S1 for details. All the films were stored at 25 °C and 50 % RH for 48 h before testing. The characterization methods of Alg active films were given in the Supplementary Information.

The significant differences in thickness, opacity, color parameters, water contact angle and mechanical properties were determined with statistical analysis. The mean differences of Alg active films were determined by analysis of variance (one-way ANOVA) in the data analysis module (Excel, Microsoft Office Professional Plus 2010). The level of significance was P < 0.05. All of the figures were graphed with Origin 2018.

TEM is uesd to study the morphology and structure of CGA-CDs, as shown in Fig. 1 . From the TEM images (Fig. 1(a and b)), it can be seen that CGA-CDs show very good dispersion in water, showing a typical spherical shape. According to the size statistics of these CGA-CDs (as shown in Fig. 1c), it can be found that the particle size of CGA-CDs is mainly concentrated in the range of 1.6–2.4 nm, and the average particle size is ∼2.0 nm, which indicates that the particle size distribution of CGA-CDs is narrow. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1b inset) shows that CGA-CDs have a high crystal structure, and the plane spacing of lattice fringes is ∼0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) diffraction plane of graphene [12], which indicates that CGA-CDs have a graphitized structure.

To investigate the chemical structure of CGA-CDs, the synthetic CGA-CDs are characterized by FT-IR, UV–Vis spectroscopy and XPS, as shown in Fig. 2 . In the infrared spectrum of CGA, the infrared absorption peaks at 3466 and 3325 cm−1 are caused by the stretching vibration of -OH, the absorption peak at 1685 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of CO, and the stretching vibration peak of aromatic ring appears at 1599, 1516, 1440 cm−1 (orange marked area) [26,27]. The copolymerization of CGA and PEI occurs through Michael addition reaction, and the conversion of primary amine to secondary amine is a key indication [12]. According to the infrared spectrum of CGA-CDs, the bending vibration peak of NH and the stretching vibration peak of CN in secondary amines appear at 1574 and 1359 cm−1, respectively [12,28]. CGA-CDs do not have the infrared absorption peaks of the orange marked area, which indicates that the aromatic structure of CGA-CDs is significantly reduced. The results of FT-IR analysis preliminarily reveal that CGA-CDs are synthesized via Michael addition reaction between CGA and PEI.

It can be seen from Fig. 2b that CGA has obvious UV absorption peaks at 217 and 326 nm, which is closely related to ester bonds, unsaturated double bonds and polyphenols in the molecular structure of CGA [26]. The characteristic absorption peak of CGA-CDs only appears near 285 nm, which is related to the change of CGA structure, which might be contributed by the π–π* electronic transition of CO groups [12]. Both CGA and CGA-CDs exhibit excellent UV light absorption due to the active groups in their structures. In the high energy blue light range (HEBL, 400–500 nm), CGA shows very low absorbance, which means that CGA has no absorption in the HEBL range. However, CGA-CDs still maintain a certain light absorption in HEBL range. Therefore, CGA-CDs can not only have good UV barrier properties, but also absorb a certain HEBL, so as to more effectively protect the packaged food [29].

XPS is further used to confirm the incorporation of nitrogen into the structure of CGA-CDs, as shown in Fig. 2(c–f). XPS elemental analysis results show that the mass fraction of C, O, and N elements in CGA-CDs is 66.9 %, 18.8 %, and 14.4 %, respectively. According to the molecular formula of CGA, the mass fractions of C and O elements can be calculated to be 54.2 % and 40.7 %, respectively. According to the molecular formula of PEI, the mass fractions of C and N elements can be calculated to be 55.8 % and 32.6 %, respectively. Comparing the mass fractions of elements in CGA, PEI, and CGA-CDs, it can be observed that N element content significantly increases after the Michael addition reaction between CGA and PEI. Further analysis of XPS spectra for CGA-CDs reveals that energy peaks corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s appear near 283.2, 397.2, 529.2 eV in Fig. 2c [30]. As shown in Fig. 2d, the high-resolution spectrum of C1s consists of four peaks: 283.7 eV(aromatic CC), 284.6 eV (aliphatic CC and CH), 285.5 eV (aliphatic CN), and 287.0 eV (aromatic CN and aromatic C-OH (phenolic hydroxyl)) [30]. As shown in Fig. 2f, the high-resolution spectrum of O1s contains three peaks: 530.0 eV (aromatic CO), 530.9 eV (C-OH), and 532.2 eV (C-O-C) [13]. As shown in Fig. 2f, the high-resolution spectrum of N1s has two main peaks appeared at 398.7 and 401.5 eV, which are related to C-NH2 (amino N) and N-(C)3 (graphic N), respectively [31,32]. From the test results of zeta potential, Zeta potential of CGA-CDs is +32.0 ± 0.6 mV, which indicates that the surface of CGA-CDs carries a large amount of positive charge, which may be related to the amino groups on the surface of CGA-CDs. Zeta potential (absolute value >30 mV) of CGA-CDs reveals that the particles may have good colloidal stability [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical property and cytotoxicity of CGA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence testing was performed on CGA-CDs using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . The excitation and emission slits were set to 20 and 10 nm respectively. When CGA-CDs is excited at wavelengths from 279 nm to 305 nm (2 nm interval), PL (photoluminescence) emission peak shifts 279 nm to 305 nm (dissolved in water). The maximum PL emission is observed at the wavelength of 585 nm when CGA-CDs is excited at 293 nm, which indicates that the (PL) intensity of CGA-CDs is closely related to the excitation wavelength. This excitation-dependent PL behavior is extensively reported in fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials, and it may result from optical selection and surface defects of CGA-CDs [34]. UV–Visible absorption and fluorescence spectra of CGA-CDs are obtained to study the optical properties of CGA-CDs, as shown in Fig. 3b. From UV–Vis spectra of CGA-CDs, it can be seen that CGA-CDs exhibit significant UV light absorption around 285 nm. The UV absorption peak of CGA-CDs is related to the π electron transition in oxygen-containing CGA-CDs, which can be attributed to the π-π* transition in the CO groups [12,35]. The excitation wavelength of CGA-CDs is 293 nm, while the center of the peak in the emission spectrum is at 585 nm. CGA-CDs solution emits light green fluorescence under the conventional UV lamp (365 nm), which may be derived from the immobilization of the sub-fluorophore in CGA-CDs via the covalent bond and/or supramolecular interactions, leading to the enhancement of radiative transition, namely the crosslink-enhanced emission effect [12,36].

Considering the safety of food packaging applications, the safety of CGA-CDs can be evaluated using cytotoxicity experiments using MTT assay [36]. As can be seen from Fig. 3c, L929 cells (mouse fibroblasts cells) are cultured with CGA-CDs of different concentrations for 24 h, and the cell viability of L929 cells remains above 80 % at high concentrations (up to 0.5 mg/mL). Especially when the concentration of CGA-CDs was between 0 and 0.1 mg/mL, the cell viability of L929 cells exceeds 90 %, which reflects CGA-CDs with low cytotoxicity and safety can provide safety guarantee for its application in food contact materials [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical structure of Alg active films
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical structure of Alg active films is studied by FT-IR spectra, as shown in Fig. 4a. It can be seen that pure Alg film shows all the infrared adsorption characteristic peaks of sodium alginate reported in the literature [13,38,39]. The absorption peak at 3264 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups in Alg molecular chain, and the absorption peaks at 1596 and 1408 cm−1 are attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of CO in carboxyl groups, respectively. Meanwhile, the absorption peaks at 1083 and 1021 cm−1 are caused by the C-O-C stretching vibration. Due to the significant overlap between the infrared absorption in CGA-CDs and Alg matrix, there is no significant change between Alg active films and pure Alg film. However, it is worth noting that the infrared absorption peak of the stretching vibration for -OH groups in Alg active films has a red shift from 3264 to 3256 cm−1, which is caused by the combined action of the stretching vibration of -OH and NH groups by the addition of CGA-CDs [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical property of Alg active films
ce:section.ce:para: UV/HEBL barrier property of packaging films/coatings is a crucial factor in food packaging applications. It serves to minimize UV/HEBL-induced lipid oxidation, preserve the flavor of packaged foods, prevent nutrient losses, discoloration, and off-flavors, thereby extending the shelf life of food products [29,41,42]. UV–Vis light transmittance curves and digital photos of Alg active films are shown in Fig. 4(b and c). Optical parameters of Alg active films are presented in Table 1 . As can be seen in Fig. 4b, after the addition of LDHs@CGA and CGA-CDs, there is a significant change in the UV–visible absorption characteristics of Alg active films. Especially in the UV range (190–400 nm), the transmittance of UV light significantly decreases with the addition of CGA-CDs. When the amount of CGA-CDs reaches 2 %, the UV transmittance (280 nm) of Alg active films is only 2.66 %, which is reduced by 88.1 % compared with pure Alg film. In the visible light range (400–800 nm), when the amount of CGA-CDs is only 0.5 %, the light transmittance of Alg active films (at 600 nm) decreases to 46.93 %, which is only 10.7 % lower than that of pure Alg film. The addition of CGA-CDs results in a certain degree of decrease in the light transmittance of Alg active films. It is worth mentioning that in the HEBL range, the addition of CGA-CDs reaches 2 %, the light transmittance of CLA-2 (at 400 nm) is 60.1 % lower than that of pure Alg film. This indicates that the addition of CGA-CDs has a much higher absorption of UV/HEBL light than visible light, ensuring that Alg active films exhibit excellent UV/HEBL barrier properties without affecting their visibility. From the digital photos of Alg active films (Fig. 4c), it can be seen that the color of Alg active films changes from colorless and transparent to yellow brown with the addition of CGA-CDs. However, all the films maintain good transparency, and the graphics behind the digital photos can be clearly displayed. The opacity of Alg active films can be calculated based on the absorbance at 600 nm and the thickness of the films, as shown in Table 1. From Table 1, it can be seen that the opacity of Alg active films is between 5.42 and 6.14, and the opacity value is not high, indicating that all the films have a certain degree of transparency, which is consistent with the phenomenon observed in the digital photos.

In order to further investigate the specific color change trend of Alg active films, the colorimeter is further used to analyze the effect of CGA-CDs on the color of Alg matrix, as presented in Table 2 . With the addition of CGA-CDs, the value of L* gradually decreases, while the values of a* and b* significantly increase (P < 0.05). This indicates that the brightness of Alg active films has decreases to a certain extent, and Alg active films change significantly in the red and yellow directions. The color in digital photos also shows a yellow brown trend. In order to evaluate the color change of Alg active films, the browning index (BI) and whiteness index (WI) to visually display the color change of Alg active films (P < 0.05). From the calculation results in Table 2, it can be seen that with the addition of CGA-CDs, Alg active films shows browning, and the increase in BI is significantly higher than the decrease in WI. Therefore, although the introduction of CGA-CDs has a certain impact on the color of Alg matrix, it significantly improves the UV barrier properties of Alg matrix and does not significantly deteriorate transparency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability of Alg active films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows the thermogravimetric (TG) and derivative thermogravimetric (DTG) curves of Alg active films. The thermal analysis results of Alg active films are presented in Table 3 . It can be seen that the temperature at 5 % mass loss (T -5%) and the temperature at 50 % mass loss (T -50%) of pure Alg film are only 50.2 and 237.4 °C respectively, indicating poor thermal stability of pure Alg film. After the addition of LDHs@CGA, T -5% and T -50% of LA increase to 73.8 and 254.3 °C, respectively. According to the thermal analysis results (Table 3), the addition of LDHs@CGA significantly improved the thermal stability of pure Alg film, which is due to the layered structure of LDHs@CGA can provide good insulation and slow down the initial decomposition rate of Alg matrix, and LDHs@CGA also have good LDHs@CGA stability. With the addition of CGA-CDs, T -5% of Alg active films first decreases and then increases. This is because the initial thermal stability of CGA-CDs is not as good as that of LDHs@CGA. However, T -5% of Alg active films is still higher than that of pure Alg film. It is worth noting that when the addition of LDHs@CGA reaches 1 %, although T -5% of CLA-1 is still lower than LA. And T -50% of CLA-1 reaches 323.4 °C, which is 69.1 °C higher than LA. This may be due to the interaction among CGA-CDs, LDHs@CGA and Alg matrix. CGA-CDs and LDHs@CGA synergistically delay the thermal degradation of Alg matrix. From Fig. 5b, it can be seen that the addition of CGA-CDs significantly reduces the peak of the first weight loss peak (marked area, T max1) of Alg matrix, indicating that the thermal degradation rate can be well suppressed in the early stage. As the temperature increases, the peak intensity of the second weight loss peak (marked area, T max2) significantly increases, indicating that the initially non thermally degraded part also rapidly degrades after the temperature increases, but the peak position of the second weight loss peak is delayed. These results reveal that the addition of CGA-CDs can improve the thermal stability of Alg matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology and properties of Alg active films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 shows SEM images of surface for Alg active films. From Fig. 6a, it can be seen that the surface of pure Alg film exhibits a certain degree of roughness. The addition of LDHs@CGA has no significant effect on the surface morphology of Alg matrix. With the addition of CGA-CDs, the surface of Alg active films appears smoother, indicating that the addition of CGA-CDs can improve the fluidity of film-forming solutions and enhance the leveling of Alg matrix. In addition, from Fig. 6d (inset), it can be seen that when the addition of CGA-CDs reaches 2 %, there is a certain amount of protrusion on the CLA-2 surface, which may be caused by spherical CGA-CDs.

Water contact angle test can be used to study the surface properties of Alg active films, as shown in Fig. 7a. Water contact angle of pure Alg film is only 46.4°, showing strong hydrophilicity. After the addition of LDHs@CGA, the water contact angle of LA is increased, which indicates that the hydrophilicity of Alg matrix is decreased to a certain extent (P ≥ 0.05). With the addition of CGA-CDs, the hydrophilicity of Alg active films is decreased significantly (P < 0.05). When the addition of CGA-CDs reaches 2 %, the water contact angle is increased to 101.8°, showing a certain degree of hydrophobicity. Combined with surface SEM test results, after the addition of CGA-CDs, the surface of Alg active films becomes smoother, which reduces the contact area with water droplets. Meanwhile, the physical crosslinking network (hydrogen bonds) formed by CGA-CDs, LDHs@CGA and Alg matrix helps to slow down the instantaneous wetting effect of water droplets. Therefore, the addition of CGA-CDs helps to improve the hydrophobicity of Alg matrix and reduce its adsorption of water molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of Alg active films
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanical properties of Alg active films are shown in Fig. 7(b and c). As can be seen from Fig. 7(b and c), the addition of LDHs@CGA increases the elongation at break of Alg matrix to a certain extent, but has little effect on the tensile strength of Alg matrix. With the addition of CGA-CDs, the elongation at break of Alg active films shows a significant increase trend. When the amount of CGA-CDs reaches 0.5 %, the elongation at break of CLA-0.5 increases to 69.9 %, with an increase of 46.8 % compared with pure Alg film, indicating that the flexibility of Alg matrix is greatly improved (P < 0.05). With the addition of CGA-CDs, the elongation at break of Alg active films decreases to a certain extent, but is still higher than that of pure Alg film. However, when the addition of CGA-CDs increases from 0.5 % to 2 %, the tensile strength of Alg active films do not change significantly (P ≥ 0.05). It is worth mentioning that the addition of LDHs@CGA significantly enhances the tensile strength of Alg matrix, which is due to the good elastic modulus of layered clay [24]. The variation of tensile strength of Alg active films is completely different from the elongation at break. This is due to the strong hydrogen bonding between CGA-CDs, LDHs@CGA and Alg matrix, which weakens the interaction between Alg molecular chains and increases the flexibility of Alg matrix [13]. Considering the practicability and limitations of Alg, Alg based active packaging materials developed in this study are more suitable as packaging base film coatings (or edible coatings) with appropriate amounts of CGA-CDs (0.5 %–1 %). Therefore, the strength requirement of packaging material in this study is not so high compared with the conventional packaging film, and it needs good flexibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activities of Alg active films
ce:section.ce:para: For the possibility of using CGA-CDs in the food packaging, it is necessary to study the specialty and its ability against microbes, which in turn is a major factor in food spoilage. [43]. Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli) and Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus) are used as the food-borne bacteria objects of antibacterial experiments to prove the broad-spectrum antibacterial activity of Alg active films. Fig. 8 shows antibacterial activity against E. coli and digital photos of antibacterial test for LDHs@CGA, CGA-CDs and Alg active films. From Fig. 8a, it can be seen that the antibacterial rate of LDHs@CGA reaches 62.8 %, showing certain antibacterial activity. The antibacterial rate of CGA-CDs reaches 99.2 %, demonstrating excellent antibacterial activity. Pure Alg film has almost no antibacterial activity, and the antibacterial rate is only 8.5 %. After adding LDHs, the antibacterial activity of LA is only 24.0 %. With the addition of CGA-CDs, the antibacterial rate of Alg active films increases significantly. When the addition of CGA-CDs reaches 2 %, the antibacterial rate of Alg active films can reach the maximum of 86.0 %, showing excellent antibacterial activity. It can also be seen from Fig. 8b that after the addition of CGA-CDs, the number of colonies of Alg active films decrease significantly. Similar to the antibacterial experiment results of E. coli, the addition of CGA-CDs can enhance the antibacterial activity of Alg active films against S. aureus, significantly preventing their proliferation, as shown in Fig. S2. According to literature reports [9,30,44], firstly, CDs can adsorb onto the cell walls of bacteria and fungi through diffusion and electrostatic interactions, thereby hindering their nutrient uptake. Secondly, CDs can penetrate cell membranes, inhibit the formation of bacterial biofilms, and induce cytoplasmic leakage. Thirdly, CDs can also infiltrate bacterial walls and membranes, binding to DNA and RNA in bacteria and fungi via noncovalent interactions. This binding alters the structure of DNA, leading to secondary conformational changes, and causes the separation of the DNA double helix. These results indicate that CGA-CDs can effectively inhibit the reproduction and metabolism of microorganisms and play an important antibacterial role in Alg active films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Freshness preservation of Alg active films
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh-cut apples are difficult to preserve because cutting increases the growth, softening, and respiration rate of microorganisms, making them prone to oxidation and browning when exposed to air [45]. Browning is mainly caused by enzymatic oxidation, including the oxidation and polymerization of phenolic compounds by polyphenol oxidase (PPO) [46]. When cells are damaged and intracellular partitions are disrupted, PPO activity increases, leading to the release and oxidation of phenolic substrates under aerobic conditions, causing browning in different fruits and vegetables [45,46]. Meanwhile, it is also susceptible to microbial contamination during storage, leading to mold growth. Therefore, fresh-cut apples can be used as experimental subjects to study the preservation effect of Alg active coatings. In the preservation experiment, the fresh-cut apples are coated with an active coating solution using a brush coating method (using the film-forming solution during the film preparation process as Alg active coating), and the changes of fresh-cut apples at different storage times are recorded through digital camera and colorimeter.

Fig. 9 shows digital photos of fresh-cut apples at storage different times. It can be observed that fresh cut apples treated with both Blank and Alg samples show significant browning and moldy spots after 96 h of storage, indicating that pure Alg coating does not have a visible preservation effect. Fresh-cut apples treated with LA coating also showed significant browning and moldy spots after 96 h of storage, but the degree of spoilage is significantly lower than that of Blank and Alg samples. Throughout the storage process, the degree of browning and moldy spots in fresh-cut apple treated with CLA-1 coating is the lightest among all the samples. Even after a storage time of 96 h, CLA-1 sample still maintains slight browning and mold growth. This directly indicates that the addition of CGA-CDs in the Alg active coating has an inhibitory effect on fruit mildew and browning.

A colorimeter is further used to analyze the color of fresh-cut apples treated with CLA-1 coating, and obtained the variation patterns of browning index (BI) and whiteness (WI), as shown in Fig. 10 . It can be seen that both BI and WI do not show significant color changes within 1–48 h, which is consistent with the digital photos in Fig. 9. After 96 h of storage, BI of CLA-1 sample is only 62.3, significantly lower than that of blank sample (100.3), Alg sample (89.7), and LA sample (90.7). This indicates that CLA-1 coating can effectively inhibit the browning of fresh-cut apples. Therefore, CGA-CDs/LDHs@CGA/Alg active coating can effectively inhibit the oxidative browning of fresh-cut apples, and exert antibacterial and antioxidant activities.

In this work, CGA-CDs was synthesized mildly via Michael addition reaction between CGA and PEI. Alg active films were fabricated by solution casting method using Alg matrix, CGA-CDs and LDHs@CGA. The results showed that the average particle size of the synthesized CGA-CDs was ∼2.0 nm, with obvious graphitized structure inside and active groups (-OH and -NH2, etc.) outside, showing obvious UV absorption. With the addition of CGA-CDs, UV barrier, antibacterial, thermal stability, hydrophobicity and flexibility of Alg active films were significantly improved. Although CGA-CDs have a certain effect on the color of Alg matrix, Alg active films still maintain good transparency. When the amount of CGA-CDs reaches 2 %, UV transmission (280 nm) of Alg active films was only 2.66 %, which was reduced by 88.1 % compared with pure Alg film. Freshness preservation experiment of fresh-cut apples showed that Alg active coating with CGA-CDs maintained the lowest browning index and mildew. Considering the practicability and limitations of Alg matrix, Alg active films developed in this work are more suitable as packaging base film coatings / edible coatings in future applications.

Long Mao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Zhiye Dong: Investigation, Data curation. Fang Dong: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xianglong Dai: Visualization, Software. Zhiming Cao: Supervision, Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52103098), Natural Science Foundation of Fujian province, China (No. 2021J05265)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2025.139738.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Natural quercetin aiegen composite film with antibacterial and antioxidant properties for in situ sensing of al(3+) residues in food, detecting food spoilage, and extending food storage times

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. He, H. Wang, Z. Chen, S. Liu, J. Li, S. Li, Natural quercetin aiegen composite film with antibacterial and antioxidant properties for in situ sensing of al(3+) residues in food, detecting food spoilage, and extending food storage times, Acs Applied Bio Materials 1 (2018) 636-642. doi:10.1021/acsabm.8b00128.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of anacardic acid incorporated biopolymeric film for active packaging applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Preethi, J.A. Moses, C. Anandharamakrishnan, Development of anacardic acid incorporated biopolymeric film for active packaging applications, Food Packaging Shelf 28 (2021) 100656. doi:10.1016/j.fpsl.2021.100656.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation and characterization of chitosan films containing lychee (litchi chinensis sonn.) pericarp powder and their application as active food packaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Jiang, Z. Luo, H. Liu, F. Wang, H. Li, H. Gao, H. Zhang, Preparation and characterization of chitosan films containing lychee (litchi chinensis sonn.) Pericarp powder and their application as active food packaging, Foods 10 (2021) 2834. doi:10.3390/foods10112834.
